title: Population density

infobox.value: Country or dependent territory | Land Area | | Population | Density |
sq mi | per km2 | per sq mi

is a measurement of [population size](/wiki/Population_size "Population size") per unit [land area](/wiki/Land_area "Land area"). It is mostly applied to [humans](/wiki/Human "Human"), but sometimes to other living [organisms](/wiki/Organism "Organism") too. It is a key concept in [population geography](/wiki/Population_geography "Population geography").[[1]](#cite_note-about-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Measurement of population size per unit area or unit volume

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/PopulationDensityMapPerSquareKilometre.png/500px-PopulationDensityMapPerSquareKilometre.png)](/wiki/File:PopulationDensityMapPerSquareKilometre.png)

Population density (people per square kilometre) by country in 2023

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/World_population_density_1994_-_with_equator.png/500px-World_population_density_1994_-_with_equator.png)](/wiki/File:World_population_density_1994_-_with_equator.png)

Population density (people per square kilometre) map of the world in 1994. In relation to the equator it is seen that the vast majority of human population lives in the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere"), where 67% of Earth's land area is.

**Population density** is a measurement of [population size](/wiki/Population_size "Population size") per unit [land area](/wiki/Land_area "Land area"). It is mostly applied to [humans](/wiki/Human "Human"), but sometimes to other living [organisms](/wiki/Organism "Organism") too. It is a key concept in [population geography](/wiki/Population_geography "Population geography").[[1]](#cite_note-about-1)
In [botany](/wiki/Botany "Botany") and [agronomy](/wiki/Agronomy "Agronomy"), it's known as *[plant density](/wiki/Plant_density "Plant density")*.

## Biological population densities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Population_density&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biological population densities")]

Population density is population divided by total [land area](/wiki/Land_area "Land area"), sometimes including seas and oceans, as appropriate.[[1]](#cite_note-about-1)

Low densities may cause an [extinction vortex](/wiki/Extinction_vortex "Extinction vortex") and further reduce fertility. This is called the [Allee effect](/wiki/Allee_effect "Allee effect") after the scientist who identified it. Examples of the causes of reduced fertility in low population densities are:[[2]](#cite_note-2)

* Increased problems with locating sexual mates
* Increased [inbreeding](/wiki/Inbreeding "Inbreeding")

### Human densities

Main article: [List of countries and dependencies by population density](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population_density "List of countries and dependencies by population density")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/World_Population_Density_Map_2020.png/500px-World_Population_Density_Map_2020.png)](/wiki/File:World_Population_Density_Map_2020.png)

Population density (people per square kilometre) map of the world in 2020

Population density is the number of people per unit of area, usually transcribed as "per square kilometre" or square mile, and which may include or exclude, for example, areas of water or glaciers. Commonly this is calculated for a [county](/wiki/County "County"), [city](/wiki/List_of_cities_proper_by_population_density "List of cities proper by population density"), [country](/wiki/Country "Country"), another [territory](/wiki/Territory "Territory") or the [entire world](/wiki/Earth "Earth").

[World population](/wiki/World_population "World population") is around 8,000,000,000[[3]](#cite_note-3) and the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s total area (including [land](/wiki/Land "Land") and water) is 510,000,000 km2 (200,000,000 sq mi).[[4]](#cite_note-4) Therefore, the worldwide human population density is approximately 8,000,000,000 ÷ 510,000,000 = 16/km2 (41/sq mi). However, if only the Earth's land area of 150,000,000 km2 (58,000,000 sq mi) is taken into account, then human population density is 53/km2 (140/sq mi). This includes all continental and island land area, including [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"). However, if Antarctica is excluded, then population density rises to over 58 per square kilometre (150/sq mi).[[1]](#cite_note-about-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Vegetation.png/500px-Vegetation.png)](/wiki/File:Vegetation.png)

World environments map provided for comparison with maps above

The European Commission's [Joint Research Centre](/wiki/Joint_Research_Centre "Joint Research Centre") (JRC) has developed a suite of (open and free) data and tools named the [Global Human Settlement Layer](/wiki/Global_Human_Settlement_Layer "Global Human Settlement Layer") (GHSL) to improve the science for policy support to the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") Directorate Generals and Services and as support to the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") system.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Several of the most densely populated territories in the world are [city-states](/wiki/City-state "City-state"), [microstates](/wiki/Microstate "Microstate") and urban [dependencies](/wiki/Dependent_territory "Dependent territory").[[6]](#cite_note-unpop-6)[[a]](#cite_note-7) In fact, 95% of the world's population is concentrated on just 10% of the world's land.[[7]](#cite_note-8) These territories have a relatively small area and a high [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization") level, with an economically specialized [city](/wiki/City "City") population drawing also on rural resources outside the area, illustrating the difference between high population density and [overpopulation](/wiki/Human_overpopulation "Human overpopulation").

Deserts have very limited potential for growing crops as there is not enough rain to support them. Thus, their population density is generally low. However, some cities in the Middle East, such as [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai"), [Abu Dhabi](/wiki/Abu_Dhabi "Abu Dhabi") and [Kuwait City](/wiki/Kuwait_City "Kuwait City"), have been increasing in population and infrastructure growth at a fast pace.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Mongolian_Steppes_%286228096139%29.jpg/250px-Mongolian_Steppes_%286228096139%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mongolian_Steppes_(6228096139).jpg)

Mongolian Steppes. [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia") is the least densely populated country in the world due to its harsh climate as a result of its geography.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

Cities with high population densities are, by some, considered to be overpopulated, though this will depend on factors like quality of housing and infrastructure and access to resources.[[10]](#cite_note-11) Very densely populated cities are mostly in Asia (particularly [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia")); Africa's [Lagos](/wiki/Lagos "Lagos"), [Kinshasa](/wiki/Kinshasa "Kinshasa"), and [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"); South America's [Bogotá](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1 "Bogotá"), [Lima](/wiki/Lima "Lima"), and [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"); and [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City") and [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg") also fall into this category.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Monaco_by_night.JPG/250px-Monaco_by_night.JPG)](/wiki/File:Monaco_by_night.JPG)

[Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco") is currently the most densely populated nation in Europe.

City population and especially area are, however, heavily dependent on the definition of "urban area" used: densities are almost invariably higher for the center only than when suburban settlements and intervening rural areas are included, as in the [agglomeration](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") or [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") (the latter sometimes including neighboring cities).

In comparison, based on a world population of 8 billion, the world's inhabitants, if conceptualized as a loose crowd occupying just under 1 m2 (10 sq ft) per person (cf. [Jacobs Method](/wiki/Jacobs_Method "Jacobs Method")), would occupy an area of 8,000 square kilometres (3,100 sq mi) a little less than the land area of [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), 8,868 square kilometres (3,424 sq mi).

### Other methods of measurement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Population_density&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Other methods of measurement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/EU_Pop2008_1024.PNG/500px-EU_Pop2008_1024.PNG)](/wiki/File:EU_Pop2008_1024.PNG)

This population [cartogram](/wiki/Cartogram "Cartogram") of the European Union (2007–2012) uses areas and colors to represent population.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Living_population_density.png/500px-Living_population_density.png)](/wiki/File:Living_population_density.png)

Living population density by country

Although the arithmetic density is the most common way of measuring population density, several other methods have been developed to provide alternative measures of population density over a specific area.

* **Arithmetic density**: The total number of people / area of land
* **[Physiological density](/wiki/Physiological_density "Physiological density")**: The total population / area of [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land")
* **Agricultural density**: The total *rural* population / area of [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land")
* **Residential density**: The number of people living in an urban area / area of residential land
* **[Urban density](/wiki/Urban_density "Urban density")**: The number of people inhabiting an urban area / total area of urban land
* **Ecological optimum**: The density of population that can be supported by the natural resources
* **[Population weighted density](/wiki/Population_weighted_density "Population weighted density")**: Also known as **living density**, population density at which the average person lives[[13]](#cite_note-16)
